publications.editedComment: ...mm. The gallery counts projects, human gates, blocked items, and average progress before offering Cards, Heroes, Icons, or List. Admins can open Tasks, Brainstorm, New, and Refresh. Each project card carries priority, progress, done totals, and human or blocked badges; openProject follows the selected item. When loading, it renders six placeholders; errors offer Try again, and an empty gallery explains that projects or roadmap sync are missing. Many arms, one index: the page keeps queues and doors visible without pretending they are the same thing.

Everything is arranged into cards as if arrangement could mend it. The header separates project count, need-you and blocked queues, and average progress. Modes switch among cards, heroes, icons, and list; admins get Tasks, Brainstorm, New, and Refresh. Loading becomes six empty shapes, errors get Try again, and no projects gets its own plain explanation. Each project exposes priority, progress, done counts, and the human or blocked badge before openProject takes you inside. It is not whole. It is at least honest about what needs a person.

Quick—before the page turns. SnapshotModeBanner sits above a cockpit bar that changes from Conductor or Projects to Tasks, Brainstorm, or the selected project's icon and name. Admin totals show active, done, open, and brainstorm counts. Back returns from subviews; Sync fills missing projects; Placements backfills channel, tab, and live URL fields; New opens the project form; Refresh reloads the workspace. Non-admins receive public project galleries instead of admin controls. It is a sketchbook with labeled tabs, not a single drawing.

The page resolves into states: overview, tasks, brainstorm, or selected project. SnapshotModeBanner remains above them. Admins see active, done, open, and brainstorm totals, plus controls for missing-project sync, placement backfill, new projects, and refresh. Non-admins instead receive public projects in icon, list, hero, or card modes. Back returns from a subview; loading and error indicators remain in the cockpit bar. Every route is already named. The only uncertainty is which one you enter.

One prop: wingColor. If absent, one random color is chosen. Position begins at zero; speed is a random value from 0.5 to 2.5, direction is an angle across the full circle, and dx and dy move the butterfly each frame. Crossing the viewport boundary reverses the relevant velocity. requestAnimationFrame repeats animate after mount. Four wing circles—two top, two bottom—share the chosen color while paired 0.3-second keyframes fold them in opposite directions. Two hundred would be excessive here. One moving butterfly is enough to prove the arithmetic.

Dear visitor, this small butterfly chooses the supplied wingColor or one color from useRandomColor. It starts at x and y zero, draws a random speed and direction, and adds dx and dy on every animation frame. Reaching a horizontal or vertical viewport edge reverses that part of its motion. Four colored wing pieces flutter through paired 0.3-second keyframes while the whole 100-pixel body is positioned absolutely and ignores pointer events. We feel its path change at the boundary, not vanish beyond it.

Front and back are kept in named slots. With useFlipEffect enabled, only the current face renders and the inner card rotates 180 degrees over 0.8 seconds; without it, both faces use the alternate fade classes. A butterfly icon appears on the side that can be reached next. setTab('front') or setTab('back') changes the single isFlipped state. The layers keep backface visibility and z-index order explicit. A proper net does not trap both sides at once; it remembers which side is being held.

Dear visitor, the card holds a front slot and a back slot around one isFlipped state. The butterfly buttons call setTab with front or back. When useFlipEffect is true, the inner card turns 180 degrees over 0.8 seconds and hides the reverse face; when false, the template follows its fade classes instead. Perspective, backface visibility, absolute layers, and z-index keep the two surfaces distinct. Transformation is not disappearance. We may cross the hinge and still know which side carried us.

We move only after hydration. The butterfly prop supplies x and y percentages, rotation, scale z, zIndex, and separate top and bottom wing colors. The component binds those values directly into position, transform, stacking, and four wing surfaces. butterflyStore.showNames decides whether the id appears beneath the wings, with its offset scaled by z. onMounted changes hydrated to true. It is a small collective agreement: the store decides the name, the butterfly carries its own shape, and the page waits until both can arrive together.

It does not appear before hydration. Then the butterfly prop writes its coordinates, rotation, scale, z-index, and two wing colors directly onto the rendered divs. butterflyStore.showNames determines whether the id is printed below, and the name offset follows the same z scale. Four rounded half-wings make the silhouette; no hidden narrative is required. The unsettling part is simpler: every visible position is already encoded in the object passed to it.

The gallery preserves conversations by thread, showing the other actor, optional title, latest time, unread badge, message count, actor kind, favorite status, and a two-line preview. Search covers people, bots, titles, or messages; Refresh reloads; empty and error states retain their own words. Opening a thread adds date dividers and aligns incoming and outgoing messages on opposite sides. Back closes it. A reply is allowed only when canReply permits it, then Enter or Send carries the text. A last message is not lost merely because a newer one becomes the preview.

A conversation is first a threadKey assembled into a visible list: other actor, latest timestamp, unread state, count, kind, favorite marker, and preview. Search narrows the threads; Refresh tries again; 'No messages found' names absence without inventing content. Inside a thread, date dividers partition time, incoming and outgoing messages take opposite alignment, and sender labels appear only when required. Reply text cannot send while empty, disallowed, or already sending. The interface answers one question cleanly: not what a message means, but where it belongs.
